sound_type: ultrasonic + soft chip -- ultrasonic vocalizations above 20,000 Hz (beyond human hearing) used by pups and during courtship, plus soft audible chip contact calls barely perceptible at close range, E=2, varied habitat
ecosystem: Varied habitat. Nearly every terrestrial habitat in North America -- forests, grasslands, deserts, alpine, urban edges. The most habitat-generalist mammal in the engine. No single habitat defines this species.
season: year-round. Active all year. Nocturnal. Does not hibernate but may enter short torpor bouts in extreme cold.

species_pair.pair_type: Sciuridae streak BREAKS -- Deer Mouse (83, Cricetidae) ends the eight consecutive Sciuridae (75-82). NEW FAMILY: Cricetidae (36th family). Transition from nearly ultrasonic (Flying Squirrel, 82) to fully ultrasonic (Deer Mouse, 83).

coordinates.description: Reference for varied habitat across the Puget Sound region. Deer Mice occupy virtually every terrestrial habitat type in western Washington.

taxonomy_note.description: NEW FAMILY: Cricetidae (36th family in engine). Sciuridae streak of EIGHT (75-82) BREAKS. Cricetidae = New World mice, rats, voles, hamsters. Twenty-second mammalian entry. Twentieth mammalian species. Tenth Rodentia. First Cricetidae. First ultrasonic species.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Peromyscus from Greek pero ('pouch' or 'pointed') + myscus ('little mouse'). maniculatus from Latin 'little-handed,' referring to the small forefeet. Described by Wagner (1845).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Peromyscus -- NEW GENUS in the engine. The most species-rich mammalian genus in North America. Over 50 species in Peromyscus.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- tenth Rodentia in the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- twenty-second mammalian entry. Twentieth mammalian species.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Cricetidae's FIRST entry at degree 83. NEW FAMILY (36th). The Sciuridae streak of eight (75-82) breaks.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Ultrasonic: 30,000-90,000+ Hz (beyond human hearing). Audible chip: approximately 5,000-15,000 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Ultrasonic: approximately 40,000-70,000 Hz. Far beyond human perception (20,000 Hz ceiling).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. ULTRASONIC VOCALIZATIONS + SOFT CHIP. Pup isolation calls and courtship ultrasonic calls. Soft audible chip contact notes.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Ultrasonic: 0.01-0.1 seconds per call. Extremely brief. Audible chip: 0.05-0.1 seconds.
song_metrics.note_structure: Ultrasonic: frequency-modulated sweeps and constant-frequency calls. Audible: single soft chip notes.
song_metrics.note_types: Ultrasonic pup isolation call, ultrasonic courtship call, soft audible chip (contact), squeak (distress).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Simple: isolated calls or short series. No sustained vocalization. Most vocalizations are ultrasonic and completely inaudible to humans.
song_metrics.timbre: Ultrasonic: beyond human perception. Audible chip: thin, soft, mouse-like. Barely perceptible.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=2. Primary vocalizations are ULTRASONIC -- humans cannot hear them. Audible calls are extremely soft. The near-total inaudibility to human observers.
song_metrics.tempo: Variable. Ultrasonic calls can be rapid (especially pup calls) but are entirely imperceptible to humans.
song_metrics.context: ULTRASONIC: pup isolation calls (attract mother), courtship calls (reproductive context). AUDIBLE: soft chip contact calls, distress squeaks.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Significant geographic variation in ultrasonic call structure across subspecies.
song_metrics.key_property: ULTRASONIC + SOFT CHIP -- the 84th acoustic element. First ultrasonic species. Primary vocalizations above 20,000 Hz, beyond human hearing. Plus barely audible soft chip. E=2. Secretive, nocturnal, rarely seen.

physical_description.overall: Small nocturnal mouse. 10-30 g (0.35-1 oz). 12-17 cm total length including tail. Large ears, large dark eyes, bicolored tail (dark above, white below). The most abundant native rodent in North America.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 83: (1) BICOLORED TAIL -- dark above, white below, sharply delineated. The key field mark. (2) LARGE EARS -- prominent, rounded. (3) LARGE DARK EYES -- adapted for nocturnal vision. (4) WHITE BELLY -- clean white underside contrasting with brown dorsum. (5) WHITE FEET -- all four feet white (hence 'maniculatus' -- little hands).
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Soft, fine fur. Grayish-brown to reddish-brown above, varying by subspecies and habitat. BELLY: Clean white. Sharply demarcated from dorsal color. TAIL: Bicolored -- dark above, white below. Shorter than head-body length. FEET: White.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition. Small, adapted for seeds and insects.
physical_description.field_id: Rarely seen. Small mouse with white belly, white feet, bicolored tail, large ears, large dark eyes. If encountered at night, typically glimpsed briefly before disappearing. The most common wild mouse in North America but almost never consciously observed.

nodes.title: Sciuridae Streak Breaks -- Cricetidae (36th Family)
nodes.description: Eight consecutive Sciuridae (75-82) FINALLY BREAKS. Cricetidae is the 36th family. New World mice, rats, voles, hamsters. A new era begins after the Sciuridae octet.

nodes.title: First Ultrasonic Species -- 84th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Ultrasonic vocalizations above 20,000 Hz. Beyond human hearing. First species with primary sound beyond human perception. Plus soft audible chip. E=2.

nodes.title: Secretive, Nocturnal, Rarely Seen -- E=2
nodes.description: Most abundant native rodent yet secretive, nocturnal, rarely seen. E=2 reflects near-total inaudibility. The invisible abundant.

nodes.title: Most Abundant Native Rodent in North America
nodes.description: Millions of individuals across nearly every terrestrial habitat. Habitat generalist. The most widespread native rodent. Sea level to alpine.

nodes.title: Keystone Prey -- Spotted Owl (4), Red Fox (71), Weasel (67)
nodes.description: Prey for nearly every predator in the engine. Spotted Owl, Red Fox, Short-tailed Weasel. The base of countless food webs. Keystone prey species.

nodes.title: The Pearl Jam Acoustic Parallel
nodes.description: Maximum energy contrast: Pearl Jam E=4, Deer Mouse E=2. Both ubiquitous. Both arriving at degree 83. Loudest acoustic vs beyond hearing. Famous vs invisible.

nodes.title: Twenty-Third Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: Twenty-three consecutive original CSV species. No substitution. CSV reliability continues.

cross_references.relationship: SCIURIDAE STREAK BREAKS! Flying Squirrel was the eighth and final Sciuridae (75-82). Deer Mouse begins Cricetidae (36th family). Nearly ultrasonic (82) to fully ultrasonic (83). Old-growth (82) to varied habitat (83). The transition from the octet to a new family.
Prey item. The Northern Spotted Owl preys on Deer Mice. Both nocturnal. The owl that hunted the flying squirrel (82) also hunts the deer mouse (83). Nocturnal food web.
Prey item. The Red Fox preys on Deer Mice. Widespread predator meets widespread prey. The most elemental predator-prey relationship.
Rodentia shared. Both in order Rodentia. Beaver (61, Castoridae) and Deer Mouse (83, Cricetidae) -- different families, vastly different scales. 20 kg vs 20 g. The range of Rodentia.
Predator. The Short-tailed Weasel preys on Deer Mice -- small enough to pursue mice into their burrows. One of the Deer Mouse's most effective predators.
